content:
# John Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JHN_021_page_001.jpg

### Jesus Appears to Seven Disciples

1. Later, Jesus appeared again to the disciples beside the Sea of Galilee. This is how it happened.

2. Several of the disciples were there — Simon Peter, Thomas (nicknamed the Twin), Nathanael from Cana in Galilee, the sons of Zebedee, and two other disciples.

3. Simon Peter said, "I'm going fishing." "We'll come, too," they all said. So they went out in the boat, but they caught nothing all night.

4. At dawn Jesus was standing on the beach, but the disciples couldn't see who he was.

5. He called out, "Fellows, have you caught any fish?" "No," they replied.

6. Then he said, "Throw out your net on the right-hand side of the boat, and you'll get some!" So they did, and they couldn't haul in the net because there were so many fish in it.

7. Then the disciple Jesus loved said to Peter, "It's the Lord!" When Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he put on his tunic (for he had stripped for work), jumped into the water, and headed for shore.

8. The others stayed with the boat and pulled the loaded net to the shore, for they were only about a hundred yards from shore.

9. When they got there, they found breakfast waiting for them — fish cooking over a charcoal fire, and some bread.

10. "Bring some of the fish you've just caught," Jesus said.

11. So Simon Peter went aboard and dragged the net to the shore. There were 153 large fish, and yet the net hadn't torn.

12. "Now come and have some breakfast!" Jesus said. None of the disciples dared to ask him, "Who are you?" They knew it was the Lord.

13. Then Jesus served them the bread and the fish.

14. This was the third time Jesus had appeared to his disciples since he had been raised from the dead.

### Jesus Reinstates Peter

15. After breakfast Jesus asked Simon Peter, "Simon son of John, do you love me more than these?" "Yes, Lord," Peter replied, "you know I love you." "Then feed my lambs," Jesus told him.

16. Jesus repeated the question: "Simon son of John, do you love me?" "Yes, Lord," Peter said, "you know I love you." "Then take care of my sheep," Jesus said.

17. A third time he asked him, "Simon son of John, do you love me?" Peter was hurt that Jesus asked the question a third time. He said, "Lord, you know everything. You know that I love you." Jesus said, "Then feed my sheep.

18. I tell you the solemn truth: when you were young, you were able to do as you liked; you dressed yourself and went wherever you wanted to go. But when you are old, you will stretch out your hands, and others will dress you and take you where you don't want to go."

19. Jesus said this to let him know by what kind of death he would glorify God. Then Jesus told him, "Follow me."

### The Disciple Jesus Loved

20. Peter turned around and saw behind them the disciple Jesus loved — the one who had leaned over to Jesus during supper and asked, "Lord, who will betray you?"

21. Peter asked Jesus, "What about him, Lord?"

22. Jesus replied, "If I want him to remain alive until I return, what is that to you? As for you, follow me."

23. So the rumor spread among the community of believers that this disciple wouldn't die. But that isn't what Jesus said at all. He only said, "If I want him to remain alive until I return, what is that to you?"

24. This is the disciple who testifies to these events and has recorded them here. And we know that his account of these things is accurate.

25. Jesus also did many other things. If they were all written down, I suppose the whole world could not contain the books that would be written.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 3 — "I'm going fishing":** Peter goes back to what he knows. The resurrection has happened, but the implications haven't fully landed. So he does what fishermen do — he fishes. And catches nothing. Seven experienced fishermen, fishing all night, catch zero fish. Jesus doesn't scold them. He shows up at dawn and makes breakfast. The risen Christ meets people where they are, even when "where they are" is back at their old jobs, empty-handed and confused.

**Verse 9 — The charcoal fire:** ἀνθρακιά. The same word — and only the same word — as the fire where Peter denied Jesus three times (18:18). This is not coincidence. This is therapy. Jesus recreates the scene of Peter's worst moment — same fire, same cold morning, same smell of charcoal — and replaces the memory. Three denials, three questions, three commissions. The architecture of restoration mirrors the architecture of failure. Healing happens in the same place as wounding.

**Verses 15-17 — "Do you love me?":** Three times Jesus asks. Three times Peter answers. Three times Jesus commissions. Each question erases a denial. But notice: Jesus doesn't ask Peter to explain, apologize, or promise never to fail again. He doesn't say "Do you feel sorry?" He says "Do you love me?" The only qualification for shepherding God's people is not competence, not theological education, not moral perfection. It's love. And love after failure is the most tested kind.

**Verse 18 — "Where you do not want to go":** The most sobering verse in the chapter. Jesus reinstates Peter and then tells him he will be crucified. The call to "follow me" now includes a cross. Peter, who once said "I will lay down my life for you" (13:37) and then denied Jesus three times, will eventually do exactly what he boasted — lay down his life. But not in his own strength. Not on his own timeline. Not the way he imagined.

**Verse 22 — "What is that to you? You follow me.":** Peter looks at the Beloved Disciple and asks, "What about him?" Jesus' answer is the last recorded command in the Gospel: mind your own calling. Don't compare. Don't measure your discipleship against someone else's. Don't ask why their path is different from yours. You follow me. The simplest and hardest instruction Jesus ever gave.

**Verse 25 — "The whole world could not contain the books":** The Gospel ends with extravagance — not just the 153 fish or the seventy-five pounds of spices but the claim that Jesus did more than could ever be written. The book is over, but the story isn't. The written Gospel is a window, not a wall. What Jesus did — and does — overflows every container we build for it.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
